retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Endocarditis -- Epidemiology -- Risk factors
retrieved_docs.content: Age greater than 60 years, male gender, injection drug use, history of prior infective endocarditis, poor dentition or dental procedure, presence of a prosthetic valve or intracardiac device, history of valvular disease (rheumatic heart disease, mitral valve prolapse, aortic valve disease, mitral regurgitation, etc), congenital heart disease (aortic stenosis, bicuspid aortic valve, pulmonary stenosis, ventricular septal defect, patent ductus arteriosus, coarctation of the aorta, and tetralogy of Fallot), indwelling intravenous catheter, immunosuppression, hemodialysis patients. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Tetralogy of Fallot -- Complications -- Long-Term Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Other long-term complications include RV aneurysm from the outflow patch or the ventriculotomy, distal pulmonary artery obstruction, ventricular hypertrophy, and aortic root dilation and insufficiency. Patients can have exercise intolerance, which can be aggravated by concomitant ventricular dyssynchrony and signs and symptoms of CHF. [23] An aortic root dilatation >55 cm with aortic insufficiency warrants aortic root surgery. [20] Patients with TOF, repaired or unrepaired, are at risk of endocarditis and should receive SBE prophylaxis before any dental or elective surgical procedure. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Tetralogy of Fallot -- Complications -- Mortality in Unrepaired Tetralogy of Fallot
retrieved_docs.content: The most frequent causes of mortality in patients with no surgical intervention include hypoxic spells (68%), cerebrovascular accidents (17%), and brain abscesses (13%). Within the first year of life, 25% of infants with severe RVOTO die if left untreated, 40% by 3 years of age, 70% by 10 years of age, and 95% by 40 years of age. [57] [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Tetralogy of Fallot -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: In general, patients with TOF require several surgeries. Pulmonary valve replacement via surgical or transcatheter approach will eventually be necessary during early adulthood. These patients require lifelong subacute bacterial endocarditis prophylaxis. [64] Most patients will also require services to help with their neurodevelopmental disabilities, which are being increasingly recognized in patients with CHD. Should a woman with TOF reach childbearing age and decide to become pregnant, a professional team including obstetricians, high-risk perinatologists, adult congenital cardiologists, adult congenital interventionists, and electrophysiologists, as well as pediatric cardiac surgeons, will be required to assist throughout the pregnancy, peripartum and postpartum period. [65]

retrieved_docs.title: Endocarditis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: It is common to find infective endocarditis as a complication of congenital heart disease or rheumatic heart disease. However, is important to mention that infective endocarditis can present in children without any abnormal valves or cardiac malformations.

retrieved_docs.title: Tetralogy of Fallot -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of these patients is also affected by the need for pulmonary valve replacement due to progressive pulmonary insufficiency, which places them at risk of bacterial endocarditis. [24] Very recently, a multinational, multicenter study demonstrated for the first time that pulmonary valve replacement (PVR) in patients with repaired TOF is associated with a lower probability of sustained ventricular tachycardia (VT) and death, especially in those patients with more severe RV volume overload from pulmonary insufficiency, who are at high risk of developing mechanoelectrical cardiomyopathy. The study, however, did not determine the best timing for PVR or whether surgery or transcatheter pulmonary valve placement yields better results. [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Tetralogy of Fallot -- Complications -- Short Term Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications within the immediate postoperative period after complete TOF repair include pericardial and pleural effusion requiring drainage, chylothorax, bleeding requiring reoperation, superficial wound infection, moderate pulmonary regurgitation in up to 14% of patients, residual ventricular septal defects with persistent hypoxemia, and RVOTO. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Tetralogy of Fallot -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: “Tet spells” can present with respiratory distress and worsening cyanosis. This clinical scenario can also be observed with other cardiac malformations with right-to-left shunting, including complete transposition of the great arteries (d-TGA) with pulmonary stenosis, double outlet right ventricle with severe pulmonary stenosis, tricuspid atresia, and Ebstein anomaly. Isolated severe pulmonic or aortic stenosis can produce a similar clinical picture. Viral or bacterial infections such as bronchiolitis or pneumonia can also be included as a differential diagnosis and pneumothorax.

retrieved_docs.title: Tetralogy of Fallot -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Due to advances in surgical correction, TOF is not an uncommon diagnosis among the adult congenital heart disease population. As these patients age, they may require reoperation or transcatheter pulmonary valve replacement, arrhythmia treatment, and lifestyle modification to prevent cardiovascular disease secondary to dyslipidemia and diabetes. [15] [59] [52]

retrieved_docs.title: Ventricular Septal Defect -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Although VSD is a distinct condition, several cardiac and non-cardiac disorders can present with similar symptoms. The differential diagnosis for VSD includes: Atrioventricular septal defect Atrial septal defect Patent ductus arteriosus Pulmonary stenosis Tetralogy of Fallot Mitral valve prolapse Tricuspid regurgitation Eisenmenger syndrome Infective endocarditis Myocarditis

retrieved_docs.title: Tetralogy of Fallot -- History and Physical -- Physical Exam Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Adult patients with TOF may have findings that include severe or free pulmonary insufficiency with RV dilatation, severe tricuspid valve regurgitation, and RV dyssynchrony from right bundle branch block (RBBB) with prolonged QRS duration from TOF repair. These factors increase the incidence of atrial and ventricular arrhythmias, which are a major risk for mortality. [23] Those rarely encountered patients who did not undergo surgical repair may have complications of chronic hypoxemia such as hyperviscosity, abnormal hemostasis, stroke, cerebral abscess, and endocarditis. [13] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Endocarditis -- Evaluation -- Possible infective Endocarditis
retrieved_docs.content: One major and one minor clinical criteria or the presence of three minor clinical criteria

retrieved_docs.title: Tricuspid Valve Endocarditis -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: IE requires management by an interdisciplinary team of clinicians, including: Internal medicine Infectious disease Cardiology Cardiothoracic surgery Nursing staff

retrieved_docs.title: Pulmonary Regurgitation -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Pulmonary hypertension and congenital heart defects, particularly tetralogy of Fallot, are the leading causes of a dysfunctional pulmonary valve resulting in regurgitation. Less frequently observed causes of pulmonary regurgitation encompass infective endocarditis, carcinoid syndrome, and rheumatic fever. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Endocarditis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In developed countries, congenital heart disease is the most important predisposing factor. In approximately 30% of patients with infective endocarditis, a predisposing factor is acknowledged and identified.

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiomegaly -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Valvular heart diseases, including stenosis or regurgitation of the aortic, mitral, pulmonary, or tricuspid valves and sub-acute bacterial endocarditis [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1454
retrieved_docs.content: Mitral valve prolapse (تدلي الصمام المترالي) and congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري) are both classified as forms of heart valve disease (مرض الصمام القلبي). Recognizing that these conditions fall under the broader category of heart valve disease is important for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 136

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis), and الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ (acute rheumatic endocarditis) are all manifestations of acute rheumatic heart disease that share a common clinical course characterized by a sudden onset and/or short duration. This abrupt and brief presentation is a key feature across these related conditions, aiding clinicians in recognizing the rapid evolution of rheumatic involvement of the heart’s structures and informing timely diagnostic and therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical association between اضطراب النظام (systemic disorder) and تدلي الصمام المترالي (mitral valve prolapse) indicates that patients who exhibit systemic dysregulation may have an increased likelihood of developing mitral valve prolapse, a condition in which the mitral valve leaflets bulge into the left atrium during systole. Recognizing this link helps clinicians consider valvular assessment in individuals with systemic disorders, facilitating earlier diagnosis and more comprehensive management of potential cardiac involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2099
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري) is classified as a type of valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي). This condition involves abnormal dilation of blood vessels that is present from birth and is considered within the spectrum of diseases affecting heart valves. Recognizing congenital vascular dilatation as a valvular heart disease aids clinicians in diagnosing and monitoring potential valve dysfunction, altered hemodynamics, and related complications such as heart failure or arrhythmias, thereby guiding appropriate management and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1884
retrieved_docs.content: Mitral valve prolapse (تدلي الصمام المترالي) is classified as a form of valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي), indicating that this condition involves abnormal movement of the mitral valve leaflets and falls within the broader spectrum of diseases affecting cardiac valves. Recognizing mitral valve prolapse as a type of valvular heart disease is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and appropriate management of patients with cardiac valve abnormalities.

Collectively, the relationships demonstrate that the heart provides the anatomical foundation for valve components, pericardial structures, inflammatory and rheumatic pathologies, and a wide spectrum of diagnostic, monitoring and therapeutic interventions, all of which are essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning and comprehensive patient care in cardiology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, acute rheumatic myocarditis (التهاب عضل القلب الروماتزمي الحاد), acute rheumatic pericarditis (الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد), and acute rheumatic endocarditis (الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ) all share a clinical course that is marked by sudden onset and/or short duration. This rapid and brief presentation is a common feature of the various acute rheumatic heart disease manifestations, indicating that clinicians should anticipate an abrupt, short‑lived disease course when evaluating and managing these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1270
retrieved_docs.content: Excision of heart valve serves as a broad procedural category that includes both the general group of heart valve surgeries (عمليات صمام القلب) and the specific subcategory labeled HEART: EXCISIONS. Within the umbrella of عمليات صمام القلب, several more narrowly defined interventions are recognized: aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري), coronary valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي), pulmonary valve replacement (إعاضة الصمام الرئوي), and triple‑valve replacement (إعاضة الصمام الثلاثي الشرف). Both استبدال الصمام الأبهري and استبدال الصمام التاجي are clinically associated with rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية), reflecting the role of this inflammatory disease in damaging cardiac valves and often necessitating their surgical replacement. Understanding these hierarchical relationships helps clinicians appreciate how general valve excision procedures relate to specific valve replacement techniques and the underlying rheumatic pathology that may drive the need for such interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 691
retrieved_docs.content: rheumatic heart disease have a chronic course and are most frequently located at the mitral valve (mitrální chlopeň). Specific entities include rheumatic heart valve stenosis, rheumatic mitral valve obstruction, and chronic rheumatic valvulitis, all of which are structural heart conditions affecting the heart valves (srdeční chlopně). Therapeutic management of mitral‑valve disease includes the narrowly defined procedure of closed heart valvotomy of the mitral valve, as well as other heart‑valve procedures performed at the valve site. Myocardial disease (اعتِلاَل عَضَل القَلب (SMQ)) is another child of the unspecified circulatory disorder and contains bacterial myocarditis (التهاب عضل القلب الجرثومي), a specific infectious subtype within the broader parent categories of peri‑, endo‑, and myocarditis and cardiomyopathy. Intraventricular pacing is a cardiac rhythm‑management intervention whose site of action is the cardiac chambers (srdeční komory), illustrating the procedural link

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2644
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية) is clinically associated with the need for aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري) and coronary valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي). The inflammatory damage caused by rheumatic fever can affect heart valves, leading to severe dysfunction of the aortic and coronary valves that may require surgical replacement. Recognizing this relationship enables clinicians to monitor patients with rheumatic fever for progressive valvular disease and to plan timely surgical intervention, thereby improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2617
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic mitral valve obstruction is classified as a structural disorder of the heart, reflecting damage to the mitral valve caused by rheumatic disease that leads to obstruction of blood flow through the valve. Recognizing this condition as a specific type of cardiac structural abnormality is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate management, and prevention of complications such as heart failure and arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 185
retrieved_docs.content: stenosis (التَضَيُّق). Surgical correction of these disorders includes closed heart valvotomy of the mitral valve and excision of heart valves (HEART: EXCISIONS). Inflammatory cardiac conditions are grouped under peri‑, endo‑, and myocarditis, with bacterial myocarditis (التهاب عضل القلب الجرثومي) as a specific entity; cardiac murmurs (نفخة قلبية) and atrial myxomas (ورم مخاطي أذيني) are additional cardiac pathologies. Procedurally, the community organizes cardiac care under “Operations and procedures.” Diagnostic interventions comprise cardiac catheterization, coronary arteriography, heart biopsy (خزعة القلب) with subtypes—open left ventricular biopsy, open right ventricular biopsy, and transluminal left ventricular biopsy—myocardial imaging, transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring, and multiple ECG‑based recordings (10‑hour portrait, fetal ECG, intra‑operative ECG, pressure, blood gases, cardiac output). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic mitral valve obstruction is a structural disorder of the heart that is classified as a heart valve disorder; heart valve disorders are located at the mitrální chlopeň (mitral valve). Consequently, rheumatic mitral valve obstruction specifically represents a rheumatic disease‑induced obstruction of the mitral valve, linking it both to the broader category of structural heart disorders and to the subset of disorders affecting the mitral valve.
